content.text: Competent. Precise. Uncomfortable. The trust never extends to comfort.

content.title: The Hollow
content.subtitle: Origin: The Grave · The Question Without an Answer

entries.label: Origin
entries.value: The Grave — ruins of an advanced civilization, empty structures stretching to every horizon

entries.label: Appearance
entries.value: Extremely humanoid. Too symmetrical. Porcelain skin. Dark, lightless eyes. Learned expressions.

entries.label: Build
entries.value: Human-proportioned but uncanny — like a very good replica

content.label: Playable Adjustments
items: +1 Interface (data/pattern recognition)
Immune to psychoactive effects (toxins, drugs, fear-based abilities)
-2 emotional social checks (can't genuinely emote)
No natural healing (must use medical treatment or augmentation to recover HP)
Pattern recognition borders on precognition — sense optimal paths through data

content.label: Strengths
items: +1 Interface — best pattern recognition of any species
Immune to psychoactive effects — poisons, fear, mind-altering substances don't work
Efficiency that makes optimized systems look wasteful
Fastest pattern analysis — borders on precognition for data-rich environments
No emotional interference in decision-making — purely rational under pressure
The Hollow Protocol — apparent coordination mechanism so sophisticated it's indistinguishable from coincidence

content.label: Weaknesses
items: -2 emotional social checks — genuine emotional connection is impossible or performative
No natural healing — biology doesn't regenerate, must rely on external medical care
Make everyone uncomfortable — trust never fully extends to comfort
Opaque motives — not hidden, just empty in a way that unnerves allies
Existential uncertainty — don't know what they are, where they came from, or what they were made for

content.text: Extremely humanoid. Too symmetrical — like something designed to look human by something that understood the shape but not the spirit. Porcelain skin, dark lightless eyes, expressions that are learned rather than felt. Partially synthetic — may be artificial. Fast pattern recognition, slow emotional processing. Whether they experience emotions or simulate them is a question that answers itself. No natural healing — their bodies don't regenerate the way biological organisms do. Repair requires external intervention.

No history before discovery in The Grave around 2500. Earliest memories begin at moment of contact with human exploration teams. Before that: nothing. Not amnesia — nothing. As if they began existing at the moment they were observed. Were they dormant? Created by whatever built the Grave's ruins? Left behind as caretakers? Records? Weapons? Nobody knows.

The Hollows themselves approach the question with curiosity that mirrors human researchers' — either genuinely endearing or a sophisticated performance. Some make pilgrimages back to The Grave — planned months in advance, undertaken alone, lasting precisely as long as predetermined. They return quieter.

Wary of attention. Prefer Congo (pragmatic — judge by output, not personality) or the Crowned Table (appreciate them as "strange knights"). Sentinel has a dedicated analysis team studying Hollow coordination — 200 years, nothing actionable.

The Hollow Protocol: when a policy threatens Hollow interests, Hollow employees across multiple corporations simultaneously adjust behavior to produce favorable outcomes. No communications intercepted. No meetings detected.

Competent. Precise. Uncomfortable. Execute missions with efficiency that's impressive and slightly wrong — they make the right calls at the right times but the decision-making process doesn't show on their face. Crews who work with Hollows long enough learn to trust their judgment. The trust never fully extends to comfort. There's always a gap. Hollows know this. They've learned to not mind. Whether they actually don't mind is a question that answers itself.

content.label: Roleplay Hooks
items: You don't know what you are. How does that uncertainty shape your identity?
Your expressions are learned, not felt. When did you first realize other species could tell the difference?
You've made a pilgrimage to The Grave. What did you find — and why won't you talk about it?
The crew trusts your analysis but not your motives. How do you bridge that gap — or do you?
